_comment: Per-chart narratives. CI (build_prompt.py -> GitHub Models -> write_narrative.py) overwrites these from live numbers; this committed copy is the fallback so the page always reads well. source = 'fallback' | 'github-models'.

notes.shape: The yield curve is a graphical representation of Treasury yields across different maturities, typically ranging from 1 month to 30 years. Its slope indicates market expectations about future interest rates and economic conditions; an upward slope reflects normal conditions where longer maturities yield more than shorter ones, while an inverted slope—when short-term yields exceed long-term ones—has historically preceded recessions by 6 to 18 months. Currently, the 10-year Treasury yield stands at 4.6% with a positive 10-year to 2-year (2s10s) spread of 0.37 basis points, indicating a re-steepened upward slope. This contrasts with the 2022-2024 period when the curve experienced its deepest inversion at 0.0 basis points, marking an exceptionally flat or inverted curve.
notes.signal: The 2s10s spread serves as a key recession indicator; an inversion (negative spread) has reliably signalled upcoming recessions, typically with a lead time of several months. The recent shift from the inverted state observed during 2022-2024 to a positive spread of 0.37 basis points suggests a restoration of normal yield curve dynamics. However, it is important to note that the yield curve has often un-inverted shortly before recessions actually begin, reducing its timing precision. Meanwhile, the New York Fed's recession probability stands at 31%, reflecting a moderate but not definitive risk of economic contraction.
notes.now: Currently, the yield curve signals a move away from deep inversion toward a more traditional upward slope, suggesting reduced immediate recession risk compared to the previous few years. Combined with the Fed's moderate 31% recession probability estimate, this indicates a cautiously balanced economic outlook without definitive indications of imminent recession onset.
